aliases: Korean Rice Bowl with Vegetables and Egg

english: Korean Rice Bowl with Vegetables and Egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 matches bibimbap as a Korean mixed rice dish; the dosirak (lunchbox) format is a serving variant not separately coded in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bibimbap (Korean mixed rice with vegetables and egg)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bibimbap or Korean mixed rice dishes in the clinical terminology system.

main_ingredients: seasoned vegetables (spinach, bean sprouts, mushrooms, carrots, zucchini)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bibimbap dosirak box weighs approximately 450g and constitutes a complete single-person meal including rice, seasoned vegetables, egg, protein, mandu, and banchan.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bibimbap (cr_id G00010272). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bibimbap dosirak as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white steamed rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the substantial vegetable, protein (egg, pork), and fat (sesame oil) content which lower the effective glycaemic response of the mixed dish. This is consistent with mixed rice dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 8.11 × 4.5) / 100 = 56 × 36.5 / 100 ≈ 20.4; using serving size 450g: carbohydrate per serving = 8.11 × 4.5 = 36.5g; GL = 56 × 36.5 / 100 = 20.4. Rounded to 20.4 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 435mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold; a full 450g serving delivers approximately 1958mg sodium, representing 85% of the 2300mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced gochujang and soy sauce, avoid adding extra condiments, and consider sharing the dosirak or consuming only half the portion to limit sodium intake to a safer level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base carries a high intrinsic GI (~64–72); however, the mixed dish GI is moderated to approximately 56 by the protein, fat, and vegetable content, and carbohydrate per 100g is only 8.11g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should monitor total portion size to manage glycaemic load, request brown rice substitution where available, and limit the quantity of gochujang paste which may contain added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 435mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat is moderate at 2.44g/100g and cholesterol is low at 10mg/100g, while potassium at 244mg/100g and dietary fibre at 1.1g/100g provide partial cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to control sodium load, retain the vegetable components for their potassium and fibre contribution, and request preparation with reduced soy sauce and gochujang to lower overall sodium content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dish contains multiple anti-inflammatory ingredients (mushrooms, spinach, sesame oil, garlic) but also pro-inflammatory risk factors including high sodium and potential red meat content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request egg or tofu as the protein instead of beef or pork, reduce gochujang and soy sauce to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling, and monitor individual response to chilli-containing nightshade ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bibimbap Dosirak is a Korean mixed rice lunchbox (도시락, dosirak) featuring steamed white rice topped with individually seasoned vegetables — typically spinach (sigeumchi namul), bean sprouts (kongnamul), julienned carrots, zucchini, and shiitake or wood-ear mushrooms — paired with a fried egg and a choice of protein such as seasoned pork bulgogi or beef. The dish is served with gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste) and sesame oil for mixing at the table. This Singapore restaurant version includes crispy pork and chive mandu (dumplings) and two banchan (Korean side dishes). Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 76 kcal, 5.5g protein, 2.44g fat, and 8.11g carbohydrate, with notable sodium at 435mg/100g. The vegetable diversity contributes antioxidants, beta-glucans from mushrooms, and potassium at 244mg/100g. The dish is clinically relevant for hypertension management due to high sodium content, and for type 2 diabetes management due to the white rice glycaemic base moderated by protein and fat. It is a culturally significant Korean everyday meal format widely consumed in Singapore's Korean food scene.
common_user_queries: Can I eat bibimbap with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010272, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. The dish identity is unambiguous and the HPB entry is an exact match.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bibimbap (G00010272) as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source was available to fill these gaps without risk of misattribution. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database mixed rice dish data; no direct GI measurement for bibimbap dosirak exists in the approved GI source. Food group reclassified from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' to better reflect the composite nature of the dish (rice, vegetables, egg, protein). Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 used for bibimbap; FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as the closest defensible match for Korean mixed rice dish — these codes are cited in good faith based on available ontology documentation and should be verified by a domain ontologist before production use.

improvement_actions_taken: Added Korean native-script name '비빔밥 도시락' to local_names.korean to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Korean cuisine.
Reclassified food_group from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' to reflect composite dish nature.
Added GI estimate (56, medium) with analogous-estimate documentation from University of Sydney GI Database.
Documented all -1 fields in notes with reason and source consulted.
Added health_context for all three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with clinical guidance.
Expanded aliases to include Korean script, English variants, and romanized forms.
Populated llm_training with 200+ character description, user queries, meal context, and ingredient synonyms.
Added ontology entries with honest gap notes for FoodOn dosirak format and SNOMED CT absence.
Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array (q8 self-check passed).
